title: Whakapapa of the Word: Storytelling from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Whakapapa o te Kupu: Te Pūrākau mai i Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: English
culturalContext: This unit is grounded in the pūrākau (story) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our kura its name. Students will explore how this awa, a vital taonga for Ngāti Kahungunu, has shaped our local identity, history, and ways of communicating. They will learn how its name tells a story of the native bats (pekapeka) that once flourished in its kōtukutuku (fuchsia) trees, connecting language, place, and memory.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret the key themes and narrative techniques within the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Compose an original piece of creative writing (narrative or poem) that uses descriptive language to reflect the significance of a local landmark.
Orally present a personal connection to a place, using effective speaking skills and integrating at least three relevant kupu Māori.
Collaboratively provide constructive peer feedback on creative writing, focusing on the use of cultural and sensory detail.

activities.title: He Kōrero Tawhito: Unpacking the Pūrākau
activities.description: Students are welcomed onto the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (or view drone footage/photos in class). A kaumātua or kaiako shares the full pūrākau of the awa. In small groups, students create a visual 'story map' identifying key characters (e.g., the pekapeka, the kōtukutuku), the setting, the conflict, and the resolution, analyzing the story's structure and core values.

activities.title: Kupu Whakaahua: The Language of Place
activities.description: A vocabulary-building workshop focusing on rich descriptive language. Using excerpts from local authors like Patricia Grace or Witi Ihimaera, students identify powerful verbs, adjectives, and metaphors. They then brainstorm and define a shared glossary of kupu Māori and English terms specific to our awa and whenua (e.g., wai, repo, manu, ngahere, kōtukutuku, pekapeka) to use in their own writing.

activities.title: Taku Turangawaewae: My Place to Stand
activities.description: Students select a personal local place of significance (their garden, a marae, a beach, a park). They draft a short piece of creative writing (narrative or poem) about this place. The writing must incorporate sensory details (sight, sound, smell) and use language from the shared glossary to evoke a strong sense of place and personal connection.

activities.title: Kōrerorero: Peer Review Circle
activities.description: In a circle format, following tikanga of respectful listening (whakarongo) and speaking (kōrero), students share their drafts. Peers provide 'warm' feedback (what they liked) and 'cool' feedback (suggestions for improvement), specifically focusing on the use of descriptive language and cultural resonance. This develops critical analysis and collaborative skills.

activities.title: Whakaatu: Our Stories, Our Place
activities.description: Students present their final polished pieces to the class in a short, formal oral presentation. They explain their personal connection to the place they wrote about and how they used language to bring it to life. The best pieces will be compiled into a class digital book to be shared with our kura community.

resources: A local kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu to share the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Digital copies of the Te Awa Tupua (Whanganui River) legislation as an example of how language defines and protects a taonga.
The online Te Aka Māori Dictionary (maoridictionary.co.nz) for kupu translation and usage.
Excerpts from 'Potiki' by Patricia Grace or 'The Whale Rider' by Witi Ihimaera that exemplify strong sense of place.

tasks: A final, polished piece of creative writing (narrative or poem) that describes a personal local place using rich sensory and cultural language.
A two-minute oral presentation to the class introducing the written piece and explaining the writer's personal connection to the place.

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and Strategies: Integrate sources of information and prior knowledge to make sense of texts.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Ideas: Select and develop ideas in a range of texts. Language Features: Use language features to create meaning and effect.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Structure: Organise texts, using a range of appropriate structures.
The Arts (Drama) - Developing Practical Knowledge: Use techniques and relevant technologies to explore drama elements.
